pattern: Counting how many subsequences of one string equal another is a two-string dp where matching characters add the diagonal count and every position carries forward the skip count.
hints: You are choosing positions in s, in order, that spell out t exactly; the question is how many distinct position sets work.
Let dp[i][j] be the number of ways the first i characters of s contain the first j characters of t.
When s[i-1] == t[j-1] you may either use that character (dp[i-1][j-1]) or skip it (dp[i-1][j]); otherwise only the skip carries forward, and an empty t has exactly one match.

edge_inputs.description: three ways to spell the shorter target
target longer than source yields zero
identical strings have exactly one matching subsequence

stress.description: long source over a tiny alphabet with a short target so counts grow large
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('ab') for _ in range(n))
    tlen = rng.randint(1, 4)
    t = ''.join(rng.choice('ab') for _ in range(tlen))
    return [s, t]
stress.note: small alphabet and short target maximise the number of distinct subsequences
